coredata.dc:title: Enzyme-free and rapid visual quantitative detection for pesticide residues utilizing portable smartphone integrated paper sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qianru
Zhang, Zhong
Xu, Shihao
Da, Liangguo
Lin, Dan
Jiang, Changlong
coredata.dc:description: Serious toxicity for organisms from pesticide glyphosate (Gly) residues to the ecosystem and human health has become a consensus. Rapid and selective detection of glyphosate, especially using a simple and portable instrument, is highly desired. In this work, we develop a novel enzyme-free rapid and visual ratiometric fluorescence sensor for selectively quantitative detecting glyphosate by integrating the designed blue carbon nanodots (CDs) and gold nanoclusters (Au NCs). The fluorescence of CDs can be quickly quenched via aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) within 2 s after introducing glyphosate, resulting from the formation of CDs-Gly-CDs complex aggregation. While the Au NCs serve as the reference signal without any change, therefore leading to obvious and instant ratiometric fluorescence variation from blue to pink to orange. The broad linear range was obtained from 0 to 180 nM with a satisfactory detection limit of 4.19 nM. Furthermore, this approach was successfully applied to detect glyphosate in real samples and a portable smartphone platform integrated paper sensor was developed for in-site visual quantitative glyphosate detection, offering a promising strategy for the construction of enzyme-free trace hazard detection system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enzyme-free
Fluorescent sensor
Smartphone platform
Visual quantitative sensing, glyphosate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and assay procedures of smartphone sensing platform
Paper sensors preparation and their application on glyphosate detection
Analysis of glyphosate in real samples
Optimization for the ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Sensitive and fast detection for glyphosate
Detection of glyphosate based on smartphone-sensing platform
Application for detection in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of (A) CDs, (B) Au NCs, (C) CDs + Au NCs and (D) CDs + Au NCs + Gly. (E) Elemental mapping images of C, N, O and Au in the as-prepared CDs + Au NCs. (F) The fluorescence spectrum of (a) CDs, (b) Au NCs and (c) CDs + Au NCs. (The inset shows the corresponding fluorescence photos.) (E) The UV–vis spectra.

(A) Fluorescence intensity change of the ratiometric sensor solution upon the addition of glyphosate (0–180 nM). The inset shows the corresponding fluorescence color photos under 365 nm UV irradiation. (B) Linear fitting plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio I450/I620 of the ratiometric sensor solution and the glyphosate concentration (0–180 nM).

(A) Demonstration of discoloration response rate experiment in sensor solution (the left part) and in test paper sensor (the right part). (B) Kinetic response of the ratiometric sensor to glyphosate. (C) The response reaction stabilized in 2 s.

(A) Fluorescence spectrum response of sensing system to various 150 nM species: Tri, Dim, Ace, Par, Chl, Mlt, Mcp, PO4 3-, H2PO4 -, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+ and glyphosate. (B) The images showing the corresponding fluorescent photos under 365 nm UV irradiation. (C) Emission intensity ratio (I450/I620)/(I450/I620)0 of CDs + Au NCs in buffer toward only the glyphosate existence or the coexistence with other species (150 nM). The red and blue columns denote coexistence without or with glyphosate solution, respectively. The green column denotes with only glyphosate.

(A) Schematic drawing for the Gly detection by using smartphone color recognizer. (B) The fluorescence color variation with the Gly concentration ranging from 0 to 180 nM (taken under 365 nm UV irradiation). (C) Linear plot of fluorescence sensor solution color change (red channel/blue channel) versus Gly concentration.

(A) Visualization detection of Gly using the fluorescent paper-based sensor. The photos were taken under 365 nm UV irradiation. (B) Plot of test paper color change (red channel/blue channel) versus Gly concentration (0–180 nM).

Schematic diagram of the detection process in samples combined with the (A) fluorescence sensing system and (B) the paper-based sensor with smartphone application. The demonstration of discoloration photos of (C) sensing solution and (D) paper-based sensor in actual testing. The table below shows the determination of glyphosate spiked in real samples.
Schematic illustration of the design of ratiometric fluorescent sensor and the visual detection principle for glyphosate.

head.ce:title: Enzyme-free and rapid visual quantitative detection for pesticide residues utilizing portable smartphone integrated paper sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui 230031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui, 230031, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui 230031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Serious toxicity for organisms from pesticide glyphosate (Gly) residues to the ecosystem and human health has become a consensus. Rapid and selective detection of glyphosate, especially using a simple and portable instrument, is highly desired. In this work, we develop a novel enzyme-free rapid and visual ratiometric fluorescence sensor for selectively quantitative detecting glyphosate by integrating the designed blue carbon nanodots (CDs) and gold nanoclusters (Au NCs). The fluorescence of CDs can be quickly quenched via aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) within 2 s after introducing glyphosate, resulting from the formation of CDs-Gly-CDs complex aggregation. While the Au NCs serve as the reference signal without any change, therefore leading to obvious and instant ratiometric fluorescence variation from blue to pink to orange. The broad linear range was obtained from 0 to 180 nM with a satisfactory detection limit of 4.19 nM. Furthermore, this approach was successfully applied to detect glyphosate in real samples and a portable smartphone platform integrated paper sensor was developed for in-site visual quantitative glyphosate detection, offering a promising strategy for the construction of enzyme-free trace hazard detection system.

ce:section.ce:para: Glyphosate (Gly), a type of common and broad-spectrum herbicide with high efficiency, has been widely used to control weeds in agriculture, urban greening, aquaculture, and other fields (Thanh et al., 2021; Clements et al., 2017). Due to long-term overuse and abuse, the residues posed extremely serious threat to the ecosystem and even human health, resulting from remaining in environments and accumulation in the human body to cause severe diseases (Turhan et al., 2020; Mesnage et al., 2015; Lesseur et al., 2021). In this context, the sensitive quantitative detection for glyphosate has attracted tremendous attention. The widely used methods for glyphosate residues monitor possess traditional lab-instruments, including ultra-high performance liquid chromatography (Pires et al., 2020), gas chromatography (Steinborn et al., 2016), spectrophotometry (Cetin et al., 2017), electrochemical method (Cao et al., 2019) and so on. However, in consideration of people's increasing requirements for in-situ, fast and portable hazard testing, these approaches can help little for on-site and rapid glyphosate residues screening owing to highly trained operators, costly apparatus, and time-consuming sample processing. Currently, newly developed biochemical sensors based on optical signals have been proven to be a promising alternative for residues determination because of their intrinsic simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity (Chen et al., 2021). Among them, some analytical strategies relied on enzyme-inhibition mechanism have been constructed for the detection from actual examples, resulting in easy optical signal recognition (Qi et al., 2022, 2021). Although these sensors have achieved potential applications in residues detection fields, they still suffer from problems. i) Most of them greatly relied on enzyme inhibition, in which enzymes are susceptible to inactivation to negatively affect the sensitivity, accuracy and practicability of sensor. ii) The operation conditions and response speed are limited by internal/external factors without realizing real-time/on-site applications. iii) The visual detection effect is quite poor and precise quantitative readout cannot be achieved (Zeng et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2021). Therefore, it is still highly desirable to design enzyme-free analytical sensors and further broaden the appealing applications.

Attributing to the continuous emergence of fluorescent nanomaterials, extensive studies have been performed for constructing more convenient and high-performance hazardous pollutants sensors (Chu et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2021). Then most of the fluorescent sensing platforms based on the electron transfer or Förster resonance energy transfer can induce fluorescent signal response and achieve the sensitive detection without the help of enzyme or aptamer. Luo et al. prepared the water-soluble fluorescent polymer carbon dots to build a pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch for rapid and selective detection of 4-Nitrophenol (Han et al., 2019). Wang et al. developed a dual-emissive nanohybrid of carbon dots and gold nanoclusters for mercuric ions determination (Yan et al., 2016). It is worth noting that as a specific type of fluorescent materials, the carbon-dots have triggered more attention recently for constructing enzyme-free rapid sensors by the virtue of ability for functional modification and quick responsiveness. Moreover, considering its good solubility, photostablity, low toxicity and easy-to-readout, the fluorescent sensing systems based on CDs have become the preferred option to construct portable devices, such as flexible paper sensors equipped with high-speed computing or high-performance cameras (Kazemifard et al., 2020; Fahimi-Kashani and Hormozi-Nezhad, 2020; Hao et al., 2020; Qin et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2018). In view of this, the fluorescent sensors based on CDs assisted by smartphone would be an excellent portable analytical device for field detection of herbicide residues (Xiao et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021).

Inspired by previous study, in this work, we design a novel and simple ratiometric fluorescence sensor for selective and visual quantitative detection of glyphosate residues based on aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) by hybridizing the superior optical properties of CDs and Au NCs. The as-synthesized CDs with specific reactive groups quickly interacts with glyphosate molecules to form complexes, accompanying with triggering blue fluorescence quenching. While the Au NCs serving as reference signal are unsusceptible to reactions with glyphosate. Thus, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor achieves the glyphosate visualization by successive fluorescence color variation from blue to pink to orange. And it can detect glyphosate with the detection limit of 4.19 nM in a wide linear concentration range, as well as acquiring glyphosate quantification by the ratio of red and blue channels (R/B) values. The portable paper sensors have been fabricated via easy-to-use method for on-site detection by color recognizer APP and smartphone with satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity. Comparing with the previous detection systems, our proposed sensor possessed three significant highlights. First and foremost, the enzyme-free sensor successfully avoid the time and strict conditions required for enzyme culture, making it more adaptable for real-time detection in various scenarios. Secondly, the sensing response completes in 2 s, which fulfills the current ever-increasing demands of rapid detection. Thirdly, with the aid of color recognizer APP, the portable integrated smartphone-sensing device via 3D printing realizes the quantitative detection result readout. As a consequence, this enzyme-free sensing strategy holds promising potential for sensitive, ultrafast and visual quantitative determination of glyphosate residues, and offers a new way for constructing reliable, convenient and on-site hazard analysis platform.

C6H5NaO7, HAuCl40.3 H2O, 11-Mercaptoundecanoic (MUA), NaOH, CH3CH2OH, polyacrylamide (M = 2–14 M), and all pesticides were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals are used as received without further purification unless otherwise specified. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) is produced with a Millipore water purification system.

PerkinElmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer was used to gather all photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Liantriant, U.K.) A JEOL 2010 transmission electron microscope was used to analyze TEM morphologies. A Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 high-performance electron spectrometer was used to collect the XPS data. A Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer was used to record UV–visible absorption spectra. Fluorescence photographs were captured using a Canon EOS600D digital camera under AGL-9406 portable UV light (365 nm).

The alterations mentioned in the preceding literature were used to synthesize the blue-emitting CDs (Wang et al., 2019). In 20 mL filtered water, 1.0 g sodium citrate and 0.52 g polyacrylamide were combined. The solution was put in a 50 mL PTFE reaction kettle and heated at 200 °C for 3 h after being sonicated for 1 h. The product was purified by dialysis (1000 Da) for 12 h after cooling to room temperature, and then dried in a 60 °C oven. Finally, the well-dispersed CDs solution was kept at 4 °C in the dark.

The Au NCs were synthesized with minor modifications based on previously published work (Sun et al., 2013; Sun and Yang, 2015). All glassware used in the experiment was immersed in freshly produced HCl/HNO3 (3:1, v/v) for 2 h before being washed completely with distilled water. First, 6.6 mg of 11-MUA was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, stirring constantly. To create a translucent solution, NaOH (100 µL, 1 M) was added to the aforesaid solution after being ultrasonicated for 30 min. Then 400 µL of HAuCl4 (1% by mass) was added into the solution, and the mixture reacted for 5 h at room temperature, during which time the colorless solution gradually became pale yellow. The final solution containing the Au NCs was purified by dialysis (1000 Da) for 4 h and filtered with the 0.22 µm microporous membrane. Finally, the clear and colorless solution was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

To detect glyphosate, we first prepared the ratiometric fluorescent sensing system by thoroughly mixing 20 µL CDs solution and 80 µL Au NCs solution in a 3 mL cuvette. This mixture was diluted with pure water to 2 mL, mixed completely, and then allowed to react for 2 min

The ratiometric fluorescent sensing system were recorded by a luminescence spectrometer. The series of glyphosate in different concentrations (0–180 nM) were added into the solution in a quartz cuvette, respectively. The mixtures were adequately blended for 2 s. Then the corresponding fluorescence spectra were collected from 350 to 800 nm after each addition under excitation wavelength at 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and assay procedures of smartphone sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication and assay procedure of smartphone sensing platform for glyphosate detection was described below. In order to achieve the purpose of on-site detection and ensure the reliability and effectiveness of the detection results, the smartphone sensing platform we designed includes the following accessories: two sample tanks (cuvette tank and paper strips tank), dark chamber, small UV lamp support, and filter (400–1100 nm) support. They were all fabricated using a 3D printer (maker bot replicator 2x). The smartphone integrated sensing platform was constructed through combining the accessories, small UV light and the smartphone. When detecting samples, the cuvette containing solution sample or paper strip immersed with analyte was placed in the sample tank, which was then loaded into the dark chamber and linked to the smartphone (HUAWEI nova2, HUAWEI Inc.). The smartphone acquired images and RGB value by using the color recognition software (Color recognizer, Xiyi Technology). The ratio between R and B, as well as the connection with glyphosate concentration, were used to achieve quantitative detection. Several image-capture points were chosen for test to achieve the average value of R/B in order to obtain more accurate detection results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper sensors preparation and their application on glyphosate detection
ce:section.ce:para: The paper strip was prepared according to our previous work (Wang et al., 2020a). First, the commercial ink cartridge was cleaned and then dried at 60 °C. The as-prepared ratiometric fluorescent sensor solution was injected into the cartridge as “ink” to print test strips by printing 20 times on non-fluorescent filter paper using an inkjet printer linked to computer. The fluorescent test paper was created and cut into strips for further use. When detecting samples, we immersed the paper strips into the solution to be assayed, and then placed in the as-fabricated sample slot, which was loaded into the dark chamber and linked to the smartphone. The color change photos of fluorescent test strip and corresponding RGB data could be captured by the inbuilt color recognition software (Color recognizer, Xiyi Technology). The quick visual quantitative glyphosate detection procedure was achieved by the smartphone integrated paper-sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of glyphosate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Glyphosate residues in the environment were commonly ingested by human through water, fruit and so on. We selected tap water, local lake water, and retail fruit juice filtered with a 0.22 µm microporous membrane without additional treatment to assess the usability of fluorescent sensors in real sample. To ensure the optimal detection effect and visualization of the sensor, we could use the PBS buffer solution to maintain optimal pH ~ 6.5 during the actual sample testing. To simulate pesticide contamination, we infused the samples with a solution containing various concentrations of glyphosate. The fluorescence spectra of spiked samples were collected by the method described above (fluorescent sensor solution and printed test paper). The data was gathered using the as-fabricated smartphone sensing platform, and then transferred into RGB values to acquire quantitative result. Three parallel trials were used to produce the average value in order to obtain more trustworthy detection result.

The construction mechanism of the ultra-sensitive glyphosate residues detection system based on ACQ was demonstrated in Scheme 1. The brief introduction of synthesis process of the CDs and Au NCs was present in Fig. S1. The blue fluorescence at 450 nm of as-prepared CDs quenched after introducing glyphosate, indicating it can serve as signal indicator. While the orange fluorescence color at 620 nm of Au NCs can be used as an internal reference fluorescence signal without change. We mixed the two fluorescence behaviors in a fixed ratio, and an obvious fluorescence color change from blue to pink and finally to orange was achieved as the adding glyphosate concentration increasing, which can be used for visual detection of the glyphosate residues. To further verify these fluorescence response behaviors, the corresponding fluorescence spectra was shown in Fig. S2. Moreover, the working principle for detection are also illustrated in Scheme 1. The rich reactive functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared CDs offers abundant reaction sites to glyphosate molecules. Upon glyphosate introducing, the carboxyl group of glyphosate can react with amino group on CDs to form ester, and the hydroxy group react with the amino group to form phosphate amide, leading to the formation of the CDs-Gly-CDs complex (Devi et al., 2017). The CDs-Gly-CDs complex induced the aggregation of CDs, accompanying with the fluorescence quench owing to the strong π-π superposition, which was also known as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) (Qian et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2020b; Huang et al., 2019; Nie et al., 2017). Whereas, the as-obtained Au NCs do not have these reaction groups, and thus stable against the glyphosate molecules. And due to the fast reaction and enzyme-free design, the whole fluorescence signal response can be completed in 2 s

To confirm the properties of CDs + Au NCs and the proposed detection mechanism, TEM, fluorescence spectroscopy, FT-IR, UV–vis and XPS measurements are carried out. First, we carried the morphological characterization of the sensor. As depicted in Fig. 1A, CDs have a distinct spherical shape with a diameter of around 3 nm and are equally distributed. The Au NCs are also equally distributed with a diameter of around 2 nm (Fig. 1B), and there was no effect on dispersion after uniformly mixing with CDs (Fig. 1C). However, it can be observed that obvious aggregation appeared after adding glyphosate (Fig. 1D). The element mapping characterization further identified the elements containing C, N, O, and Au in the homogeneous sensing system solution (Fig. 1E), which confirms the successful construction of the sensing system. The EDX spectrum of CDs + Au NCs in Fig. S3 further proved that.

Then the optical properties were also characterized. As shown in Fig. 1F, the fluorescence spectra displayed the emission peak at 450 nm of CDs and 620 nm of Au NCs with 350 nm excitation, respectively. The solution emitted pink fluorescence, as seen in the inset photos. Additionally, Fig. S4 showed the FT-IR spectra of CDs, CDs + Au NCs, and CDs + Au NCs + Gly, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum revealed that the synthesized CDs mainly included N-H (1578 cm−1), C-N (1404 cm−1), C-O (1177 cm−1) (Zhou et al., 2017). As we can see, the absorption bands corresponding to the stretching vibration of -NH2 in CDs were virtually not observed in CDs + Au NCs + Gly, indicating that the amino group of the CDs reacted with the glyphosate. A new absorption band was observed at 1316 cm−1, which may be attributed to the PO bond after introducing the glyphosate. In addition, the emerging stronger absorption peak at around 3433 cm−1 could attribute to the N-H stretching vibration of the amide bonds. These observations herein indicated that the successful synthesis of the CDs + Au NCs sensor and the CDs reacted with the glyphosate. The XPS analysis in Fig. S5 also confirmed the results of FT-IR analysis. The convoluted C 1 s spectrum of CDs revealed the existence of C-C/CC (284.8 eV), C-N (285.7 eV) and CO (288.2 eV) (Zhou et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2015). The fitted N 1 s spectrum suggested the presence of amino nitrogen (399.7 eV), pyrrole nitrogen (400.0 eV), and C-N-H (401.7 eV) (Duan et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2014). The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum verified the presence of CO (535.8 eV), C-OH (532.1 eV), and C-O (530.8 eV), respectively (Sk and Chattopadhyay, 2014). These results indicated that the CDs were rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the surfaces (Sun et al., 2006; Deng et al., 2014). After adding glyphosate, the XPS analysis still showed three typical peaks ascribing to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s with a little shift, indicating the existence of CDs + Gly, which were in good agreement with the FT-IR results. Additionally, the UV–vis data shows characteristic peaks of the sensing system in Fig. 1G. The UV–vis spectra of Au NCs had no obviously intense change after adding glyphosate. As we know, the pyrrole molecule can form large π5 6 bond. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs presented a characteristic absorption peak at 337 nm ascribed to the π–π * transition of C-C, proving the existence of pyrrole π5 6 bond (Gong et al., 2015). Then the peak disappeared after adding glyphosate, suggesting that the glyphosate introduction lead to the aggregation of pyrrole π5 6 bond. The result further demonstrated the aggregation of large numbers of the π-π bonds in pyrrole would lead to fluorescence quenching of CDs (Qian et al., 2016). The CDs + Au NCs presented similar spectra variations to those of pure CDs after introducing glyphosate. The intensity of characteristic absorption band around 337 nm decreased, indicating the ratiometric fluorescent sensor response to glyphosate. Here, we also analyzed the lifetime of the CDs. As shown in Fig. S6, the lifetime of CDs unchanged after adding glyphosate, thereby excluding the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). And we carried on the UV–vis absorption spectra of different concentrations of glyphosate (Fig. S7). The UV–vis spectra presented characteristic absorption peak of different concentrations glyphosate, which hardly overlapped with the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of as-prepared CDs at ∼ 450 nm. This result indicated that the quenched CD fluorescence was not attributed to inner filter effect (IFE) (Wang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for the ratiometric fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal experimental conditions were explored in order to attain good performance for the sensing platform. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs achieved a maximum under 350 nm excitation, while the Au NCs displays the strongest fluorescence emission under 420 nm excitation in Fig. S8. The ideal excitation wavelength was chosen as 350 nm to ensure the best visual effect of both CDs and Au NCs. The influence of the pH value exhibited in Fig. S7. The dispersion pH was adjusted using drops of HCl or NaOH of different concentrations. As shown in Fig. S9A, the fluorescence intensity of sensor around 450 nm was quite low when the pH was less than 6.5, ascribed to the protonation and deprotonation of -COOH and -NH2. With the increase of pH value, the blue fluorescence intensity increases and reaches the maximum when the pH comes to 6.5, and then decreases slightly. That is because the glyphosate molecular contains phosphoric acid group and carboxyl group. When the pH value is greater than 7.0, glyphosate is prone to neutralization reaction, which is not conducive to the amide reaction with CDs. After adding glyphosate, the pH value would also affect the detection performance and visualization (Fig. S9B). As we can see, when the pH value is 6.5, the sensor shows ideal fluorescence color change. From the above results, we can conclude that the improper pH would have negative impact on sensing sensitivity and visualization, and the 6.5 is the optimal pH value for detection. In addition, the fluorescence intensity ratio of CDs and Au NCs played an important role in the detection effect of glyphosate. As illustrated in Fig. S10, under a 365 nm UV light, an exceptionally obvious fluorescence color change of sensor solution from blue to pink could be seen upon adding glyphosate, when the emission intensity ratio (I450/I620) was tuned to 9/1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and fast detection for glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: We assessed glyphosate at various concentrations under the optimum conditions. The fluorescence intensity at 450 nm of the sensor decreased with increasing glyphosate concentration (as shown in Fig. 2A), and a successive color change from blue to pink to orange was observed in the inset fluorescence photos. In Fig. 2B, the fluorescence ratio I450/I620 showed good linear correlation (R2 = 0.9971) to the glyphosate concentration in the range of 0.0–180 nM. The limit of detection (LOD) was as low as 4.19 nM on the basis of 3δ/slope, which is lower than the maximum contamination level (MCL) of glyphosate established by the US Environmental Protection Agency and China (0.7 mg L−1 and 4.14 μM) (Rawat et al., 2016).

From the practical perspective, detection speed is crucial. As shown in Fig. 3, under UV light in dark, the response could complete in about 2 s in cuvette as well as on the as-prepared test paper, as adding glyphosate solution into sensing system. And there was no discernible difference in fluorescence intensity when contact duration was increased. Here, we also recorded the video of detection process to verify its excellent detection performance (Video S1). As mentioned above, all the performance of our proposed sensing system were able to comparably superior to some previous methods for glyphosate detection (Table S1). Compared with the instrument-based method, the easy operation and rapid response time can meet the need for portability without tedious sample pretreatment. Compared to other reported analytical methods, the considerable low LOD and wide linear range can meet the standard for sensitive detection.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.129320.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
.

The ability of the fluorescent sensor was evaluated further on basis of selectivity and anti-interference. The effect of certain common metal ions and pesticides, such as K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Trichlorfon (Tri), Dimethoate (Dim), Acephate (Ace), paraoxon (Par), Carbendazim (Car), Chlorothalonil (Chl), Malathion (Mlt) and Monocrotophos (Mcp) were tested to determine the selectivity of the assay for glyphosate detection. When injecting 150 nM metal ion and pesticides, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I450/I620)/(I450/I620)0 stayed unchanged significantly (as shown in Fig. 4A), whereas the sensor showed a notable fluorescence response toward glyphosate (150 nM). It indicated that our proposed method was extremely selective for glyphosate. The reason was that all the metal ions and the P-containing pesticides in the research (Tri, Dim, Ace, Par, Mlt and Mcp) which belong to phosphate esters possessed no appropriate reactive functional groups to CDs (Fig. S11). The CDs would be stable against to these interferences without aggregation. Moreover, we noted that there was no obvious change on the fluorescence ratio when all the interfering substances were added with glyphosate (Fig. 4B), demonstrating that the developed sensing system could provide good anti-interference capability for glyphosate detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glyphosate based on smartphone-sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: From the in-time/on-site application perspective, to develop a portable analytical device for glyphosate detection is desired. Here, we first constructed a portable platform consisting of sample tanks, dark chamber, small UV lamp support and filter support, which could connect with the smartphone. All the designed accessories were present in Fig. S12A. The operation processes of the detection by the smartphone-sensing platform were also performed (Video S2 and Video S3). Upon adding different concentrations of glyphosate, the sensing solution in cuvette displayed color change. Then we inserted the cuvette into the device (Fig. S12B). The smartphone camera was able to take series of fluorescence photos that showed color variation as the glyphosate concentration increasing from 0 to 180 nM. The color recognizer APP placed on the smartphone may evaluate the fluorescent colors and convert them to RGB values. The values of red channel/blue channel (R/B) had a linear relationship with glyphosate concentration in the range of 0 ~ 180 nM (R2 = 0.9977) in Fig. 5. As a result, the sensing platform based on smartphone showed the LOD as 10.10 nM. In this way, the visual quantitative detection of glyphosate got free from the limitation of large lab-instruments.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.129320.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S2.
Video S2
.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S3.
Video S3
.

Furthermore, to simplify the detection process, we also fabricated paper sensors. A series of glyphosate solutions with concentration from 0 to 180 nM were dropped onto the test strips and dried naturally. Then we inserted the paper into the device (Fig. S12C). The R/B value of the paper showed good linear relationship (y = 0.0063x + 0.301, R2 = 0.9964) with the glyphosate concentration in Fig. 6. And the LOD is determined to be 36.45 nM. The results indicated that this portable smartphone detection platform could achieve visual quantitative sensing of glyphosate with good sensitivity, greatly expanded the application in practical detection fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The glyphosate detection using the as-prepared sensing platform was performed in real samples (tap water, lake water and juice) by standard addition methods. Glyphosate with different concentrations was added to the pretreated samples, respectively. Detection data was recorded by fluorescence sensing solution and the paper-based sensor with smartphone application. Specific detection process was present in Fig. 7. The recoveries in three samples based on fluorescence sensing solution ranged from 94% to 96.5%, 95–98% and 101.3–102%, when the glyphosate (concentration of 50 100, and 150 nM) was independently introduced into the samples (as shown in the Table of Fig. 7), suggesting quite good accuracy of the detection. Additionally, the test strips were used to detect glyphosate in real samples. When sample solution spiked with glyphosate was dropped on the test paper, we used the smartphone application to get the RGB values. The recoveries ranged from 92% to 95%, 94–98% and 101–102%, respectively. The considerable recoveries demonstrated that the ratiometric fluorescent sensing system based on smartphone platform was capable of visual and quantitative glyphosate detection in real samples. Here, statistical analysis were also performed utilizing the IBM SPSS Statistics Program (Version 26.0). Due to the small number of data sample (n＜50), normality of the data was inspected using the Shapiro-Wilk normality test. The results showed the P value of spiked concentration ＜0.05, demonstrating the data did not conform to normal distribution. Thus, Mann-Whitney U test was used to compare continuous variables. According to the statistic, the P value ＞0.05 was considered that there is no obvious statistically significant difference between the detection results acquired using the as-fabricated sensor and the spiked concentration.

In summary, we fabricated a simple, rapid and visual fluorescence sensing platform for quantitative glyphosate detection without the assistance of enzyme. The sensing system responded sensitively to glyphosate residues, in which the blue fluorescence of CDs quenched due to the ACQ, while the intrinsic orange emission of Au NCs stayed unchanged. The successive fluorescence color change realized the visualization of glyphosate monitoring with the LOD of 4.19 nM in aqueous solution in 2 s. More importantly, we prepared paper strips assisted by the as-designed smartphone platform, exhibiting potential as portable optical analysis terminal for quantitative glyphosate tracing in real sample. Herein, the sensing platform provides a credible approach for quantitative glyphosate detection, and can be extended to other analytes or micropollution screening in the field of analytical science.

Qianru Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Zhong Zhang: Investigation, Resources, Shihao Xu: Investigation, Resources, Liangguo Da: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Dan Lin: Writing – review &editing, Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Changlong Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2021YFD2000200), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21 876 175), Key Research and Development Program of Anhui Province, China (202004d07020013), Postdoctor Research Program of Anhui Province, China (E04GEGCF).

ce:section.ce:para: Most analytical strategies for pesticides greatly relied on enzyme-inhibition mechanism, which limited their wider in-situ and on-site applications. Herein, we develop a novel enzyme-free rapid and visual ratiometric fluorescence sensor for selectively quantitative detecting glyphosate by integrating the designed blue carbon nanodots (BCDs) and gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) within 2 s, leading to obvious and instant ratiometric fluorescence variation from blue to pink to orange. Furthermore, a portable smartphone platform integrated paper sensor was developed for in-site visual quantitative glyphosate detection, offering a promising strategy for the construction of enzyme-free trace hazard detection system.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.129320.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from sodium citrate and polyacrylamide and their highly selective detection of lead and pyrophosphate
